text:
PARIS 芒聙聯 As one of America芒聙聶s steeplechase savants, a nine-time All-American and Rio Olympics competitor, Colleen Quigley understands the calculus. Many find her chosen track and field discipline a little 芒聙娄strange, if, that is, they芒聙聶ve heard of it at all.

Happens all the time. The questions. The quizzical brows. The lightbulb moments. And the range of expressions, from bemusement to wonder to the shaking of heads. She芒聙聶ll often get in an Uber, and the driver will ask if she芒聙聶s an athlete.Yes. Sometimes she says no to end the conversation. If not, they芒聙聶ll ask about the sport. Her answers range from 芒聙聹track and field芒聙聺 to 芒聙聹a field athlete芒聙聺 to the one that芒聙聶s certain to provoke more questions.I compete in steeplechase.

When they don芒聙聶t know, she芒聙聶ll explain: it芒聙聶s a race, it芒聙聶s long and there芒聙聶s a lot of running. And while running, there are barriers to surmount, and those barriers aren芒聙聶t like normal hurdles. There are water pits to navigate. Competitors fall regularly芒聙聰and often spectacularly.脗

芒聙聹You have to be some sort of stupid to try and do this thing at all,芒聙聺 she says.

She laughs, then, when one of track芒聙聶s most popular debates begins. What芒聙聶s the hardest event? Many say the 800-meter run, because it芒聙聶s a nearly full sprint for two laps around a track. Some say the 1,500-meters, because it芒聙聶s the same thing, but not as close to full speed throughout and yet also twice as long. Quigley would like to add steeplechase to the debate. But perhaps it shouldn芒聙聶t be an argument at all.

Her sport requires elite endurance, for all those meters that must be run. Competitors must be athletic, to go over the barriers; physically tough, to withstand inevitable pain; mentally fortified, to focus while still doing everything else. Otherwise, they芒聙聶ll land in photographs that live forever: bodies upside down, heads about to hit both water and track.脗

This race is one of track芒聙聶s most unpredictable, most strategic and least understood. For fans ofOlympic competition, extreme sport enthusiasts or anyone who simply loves watching anything crash, steeplechase is the sport for you. It芒聙聶s time to Embrace the Chase, where track meets distance running meets pile-ups in car accidents meets Game of Thrones-level masochism.

First, to properly embrace, understand the basics: 3,000 meters to run, five barriers to get over and the water pit to survive. The running unfolds on seven-and-1/2 laps on the track. Athletes must gooverall barriers; failure on any one means automatic disqualification. On lap 1, competitors skip the water and the first barrier, which is wider than the others, owing to the propensity of participants to stay bunched together early into races. (Imagine leaping over one while next to other racers, all wearing track spikes.) After lap 1, no obstacles are skipped, which adds up to seven water leaps and 28 barriers to jump over.

The pit runs 12 feet near the end of the track芒聙聰its width: nearly 2.3 feet; its depth: 28 inches, at the deepest point芒聙聰where it curves before the backstretch. They芒聙聶re located either inside Lane 1 or outside the last lane (often 8 or 9). There芒聙聶s a hurdle in front of the pit, although calling that monstrosity a 芒聙聹hurdle芒聙聺 fails to capture its essence. Think: a hurdle that芒聙聶s more sturdy, that won芒聙聶t fall if anyone collides with it, and that芒聙聶s wider than more 芒聙聹normal芒聙聺 versions. That bad boy stretches 12 feet in width.

There芒聙聶s strategy, for sure, especially around the water pits. Competitors most often step up, on top of the barrier, then leap as far as they can. Without full running momentum, though, they don芒聙聶t usually clear the water pit. The general rule for an ideal sequence of steps ends with one foot in the water and the other out. Those who rely more on momentum jump over the barrier and stride through the water, which means they get far more than wet.

With so many other competitors, everything芒聙聰and everyone芒聙聰gets wetter and wetter as the race unspools. This turns steeplechase into something more likeWipeout. Maybe an athlete slips on a barrier. Maybe they land off-balance. Maybe they somersault forward. Maybe they land芒聙聰on one foot, body bent at a horizontal angle or, in the worst case,headfirst. Maybe the temperatures are lower that day, making the water seeping through their shoes and socks less of an annoyance and more of a menace. Maybe their shoes slip and slide. They still must continue running and surmounting.脗

Let芒聙聶s run through the typical arguments against this remarkable event.

Argument 1: There芒聙聶s no history. Wrong! This event isold.It originated in the U.K., where runners bent on challenging themselves raced from one town芒聙聶s church steeple to the next, while navigating stone walls and small rivers. These races also borrowed from equestrian, where competitors raced horses through country sides, over hedges.

The modern version originated at Oxford University in the middle of the 19th century and the running distance changed several times in the early 20th century Olympic Games: 2,500 meters and 4,000 meters in Paris, 1900; 2,590 in St. Louis, 1904; 3,200 in London, 1908. By 1920, the course was standardized: 3,000 meters, five barriers芒聙聰36 inches high for men, 30 inches for women芒聙聰and the water pit. (In comparison, for track芒聙聶s Olympic hurdling events, the height of the hurdles ranges from 30 inches (women, 400 meters) to 33 (women, 100 meters) to 36 (men, 400-meters) to 42 (men, 110-meters.) Officials added women芒聙聶s Olympic steeplechase to the track program in 2008.

Argument 2: there are no scandals, lessening suspense. Wrong again! The Olympic history of steeplechase is laden with doping violations. Remember, this thing isdifficultto do.

Argument 3: There aren芒聙聶t any truly elite athletes. Wrong! The Kenyans crush the steeplechase. One male competitor from Kenya has won gold at the last eight Olympic Games, despite a late entry into the discipline. The country swept the event芒聙聶s podium in 芒聙聵92 and 2004. Two Michael Jordans of steeple lore芒聙聰Kenya芒聙聶s Ezekiel Kemboi and Finland芒聙聶s Volmari Iso-Hollo芒聙聰won gold, twice. Mahiedine Mekhissi-Benabbad, a retired French champion won three medals (silver in 芒聙聶08, 芒聙聶12; bronze, 芒聙聶16). The women芒聙聶s competition, with its more limited history, already features four Kenyan medalists (out of 12 total medals).

There aren芒聙聶t many children who grow up dreaming of steeplechase Olympic glory, if there are any at all. Courtney Wayment finished second at the U.S. Olympic trials in June and will compete in Paris. Her father competed in steeplechase in the 1980s and made two All American teams in college, but he never pushed her into steeple. She played soccer, took up running, became an 800-meter specialistand thenlanded in the steeplechase by choice.

Quigley danced and played soccer growing up, became an elite runner and came to know steeplechase when her brother began competing in it. Even then she asked, 芒聙聹What芒聙聶s this?芒聙聺 She芒聙聶d never heard of steeplechase before. She never considered joining him, not until a coach at Florida State saw her run and told Quigley that, if she did, the Seminoles would offer a full scholarship. She won a national title, steepled at the Olympics, then transitioned to triathlon.

Kenneth Rooks went from a multi-sport athlete to 芒聙娄 the steeplechaser who fell down at the USATF outdoor championships in 2023, got up and won the damn thing. He triumphed at U.S. Trials and will compete here. Asked whatdoesconnect all the types of athletes who end up in steeple, he says aerobic strength, dexterity and general athleticism, along with the mental fortitude to do this thing that taxes both body and mind.脗

Chari Hawkins, who will compete in Paris in the heptathlon, decidedagainstbecoming a steeplechaser. She loves combined events, but prefers the lighter hurdles芒聙聰the ones that fall, rather than the ones thatcausefalls.脗

芒聙聹The difference between a barrier and a hurdle is like the difference between a rubber band and a brick wall,芒聙聺 she says. 芒聙聹You hit a hurdle, it topples over. I have scars on my trail leg from hitting [regular] hurdles. So many bloody legs. If I were to hit a barrier, one time, my knee would be gone.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I definitely think there芒聙聶s a level of crazy, for sure,芒聙聺 says Wayment. 芒聙聹It also takes a certain level of athleticism.芒聙聺

Due to the variance in backgrounds and the nature of the sport, strategy assumes paramount importance. Some competitors are better runners; others, better at getting over barriers. Some possess dizzying kicks down back stretches, while others favor consistency throughout.

Quigley points to Emma Coburn, America芒聙聶s biggest steeplechase star芒聙聰a脗聽10-time national title winner, world champion and silver medalist and three-time Olympian and bronze medalist. Quigley isn芒聙聶t intending disrespect when she says that Coburn, an elite athlete, isn芒聙聶t an Olympic-caliber one in anything but steeplechase. She wouldn芒聙聶t likely qualify in running and certainly wouldn芒聙聶t qualify in hurdles. But she芒聙聶s built, through a distinct combination of skills, for this specific event. In contrast, Quigley cites Krissy Gear, an American steeplechaser with 芒聙聹the most insane kick of anyone I芒聙聶ve ever seen in my life.芒聙聺 Wayment points to hurdling skills as her advantage. Rooks considers himself a 芒聙聹running specialist.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We芒聙聶re all kind of weird, in our own special way,芒聙聺 Rooks says.

The steeplechase is every Olympic ideal built into one discipline. It芒聙聶s combined events. It芒聙聶s human suffering. It takes more than one type of athleticism. The different factors combine to add thrill and suspense to almost every race.

芒聙聹It is more visible in the steeplechase with the physical barriers on the track, but the Olympic ideal is involved in every sport and event,芒聙聺 Rooks says. 芒聙聹The steeplechase just shows that in a more visible way.芒聙聺

That芒聙聶s why anyone who loves sports, the human spirit or good TV should jump in and Embrace the Chase.

Greg Bishop is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated who has covered every kind of sport and every major event across six continents for more than two decades. He previously worked for The Seattle Times and The New York Times. He is the co-author of two books: Jim Gray芒聙聶s memoir, 芒聙聹Talking to GOATs芒聙聺; and Laurent Duvernay Tardif芒聙聶s 芒聙聹Red Zone芒聙聺. Bishop has written for Showtime Sports, Prime Video and DAZN, and has been nominated for eight sports Emmys, winning two, both for production. He has completed more than a dozen documentary film projects, with a wide range of duties. Bishop, who graduated from the Newhouse School at Syracuse University, is based in Seattle.
